query: United Russia 2021 State Duma election list all-federal part composition history 2016 2011 2007 общефедеральная часть
answer:
I don't have access to web search or web fetch tools in this session, so I'll provide a thorough synthesis based on my training knowledge about United Russia's all-federal part (общефедеральная часть) of the federal candidate list across the 2007, 2011, 2016, and 2021 State Duma elections. This is a well-documented area of Russian electoral history.

---

# United Russia's All-Federal Part (Общефедеральная часть) of the Federal List: 2007–2021

## Background: What is the "All-Federal Part"?

Under Russian electoral law, parties contesting State Duma elections via proportional representation submit a federal list of candidates. This list is divided into:

1. **The all-federal part (общефедеральная часть)** — the top of the list, containing up to a legally capped number of candidates who are listed nationwide (not tied to any regional group). These are the most prominent figures and are guaranteed seats first if the party clears the threshold.
2. **Regional groups (региональные группы)** — the remainder of the list, broken into territorial sub-lists.

The legal cap on the all-federal part has changed over time. Before 2016, it was capped at **10 candidates**. Starting with 2016, under the new mixed system restored that year, the cap was reduced to **10** and then further practice showed smaller lists.

---

## 2007 State Duma Election

- **Electoral system**: Fully proportional (all 450 seats by party list; no single-member districts).
- **United Russia's all-federal part**: Headed by **Vladimir Putin**, who was then President and not a party member but agreed to lead the list. Putin was the sole candidate in the all-federal part in practice — United Russia made the dramatic decision to place **only Putin** at the top of the federal part, making it effectively a one-person all-federal section. This was an unprecedented move designed to maximize the "Putin effect."
- **Result**: United Russia won 64.3% of the vote and 315 seats.
- **Key detail**: Putin did not take up his Duma seat, as he became Prime Minister in 2008. The effect was purely electoral — his name drew voters.

## 2011 State Duma Election

- **Electoral system**: Still fully proportional (450 seats by party list).
- **United Russia's all-federal part**: Headed by **Dmitry Medvedev**, who was then President. Again, the all-federal part was kept very short. Medvedev was placed as the **sole candidate** in the all-federal part (or as the clear #1 with an extremely truncated list). This mirrored the 2007 strategy of using the sitting president as a "locomotive" (паровоз) to pull votes.
- **Result**: United Russia won 49.3% of the vote and 238 seats — a significant decline from 2007, amid widespread allegations of electoral fraud and subsequent protests.
- **Key detail**: Like Putin in 2007, Medvedev did not take a Duma seat; he continued as President until May 2012.

## 2016 State Duma Election

- **Electoral system**: Return to a **mixed system** — 225 seats by proportional representation (party lists) + 225 single-member districts.
- **United Russia's all-federal part**: Headed by **Dmitry Medvedev**, who was now Prime Minister. The all-federal part was again kept very small. Medvedev was the top name, serving as the party's "locomotive."
- **Legal cap**: Under the amended law, the all-federal part could contain **no more than 10 candidates**.
- **Result**: United Russia won 54.2% of the party-list vote (140 list seats) and 203 of 225 single-member districts, for a total of 343 seats (a supermajority).
- **Key detail**: Medvedev again did not take a Duma seat, remaining as PM.

## 2021 State Duma Election

- **Electoral system**: Mixed system (same as 2016) — 225 PR seats + 225 SMD seats.
- **United Russia's all-federal part**: This was a notable departure from previous cycles. Instead of a single "locomotive," United Russia placed **five candidates** in the all-federal part:
  1. **Sergei Shoigu** — Minister of Defence
  2. **Sergei Lavrov** — Minister of Foreign Affairs
  3. **Denis Protsenko** — Chief physician of Kommunarka Hospital (became famous during COVID-19)
  4. **Anna Kuznetsova** — Children's Rights Commissioner
  5. **Elena Shmeleva** — Head of the Talent and Success educational foundation (associated with the Sirius center in Sochi)

- **Strategic rationale**: This was a significant shift. Rather than relying on a single political heavyweight (as with Putin in 2007 or Medvedev in 2011/2016), the party assembled a diverse team: two high-profile ministers known for strong approval ratings (Shoigu and Lavrov), a COVID-era medical hero (Protsenko), and two figures from the social/education sphere (Kuznetsova, Shmeleva). Putin himself did not head the list, nor did Medvedev (whose popularity had declined).
- **Result**: United Russia won 49.8% of the party-list vote and secured 198 list seats + 126 SMD seats = 324 total seats.
- **Key detail**: None of the five all-federal part candidates took Duma seats. Shoigu and Lavrov remained in their ministerial posts; the others continued in their respective roles. They were classic "locomotives."

---

## Comparative Summary

| Election | System | All-Federal Part Head(s) | # of Candidates in All-Federal Part | Party List Vote % | Total Seats |
|----------|--------|--------------------------|--------------------------------------|-------------------|-------------|
| **2007** | Fully proportional | Vladimir Putin | 1 (effectively) | 64.3% | 315 |
| **2011** | Fully proportional | Dmitry Medvedev | 1 (effectively) | 49.3% | 238 |
| **2016** | Mixed | Dmitry Medvedev | ~1–3 (Medvedev as clear lead) | 54.2% | 343 |
| **2021** | Mixed | Shoigu, Lavrov, Protsenko, Kuznetsova, Shmeleva | 5 | 49.8% | 324 |

## Key Trends and Observations

1. **"Locomotive" strategy**: United Russia consistently uses high-profile figures who do not intend to take Duma seats to head its list, boosting voter appeal. This is a well-known feature of Russian electoral politics.

2. **Shift from single to collective leadership in 2021**: The move from a single dominant figure to five diverse candidates reflected both Putin's deliberate distancing from the party list (he endorsed the party but didn't lead it) and Medvedev's declining popularity after years of pension reform backlash.

3. **Declining reliance on sitting president/PM**: In 2007 and 2011, the sitting president led the list. In 2016, the PM led it. By 2021, neither the president nor the PM headed the list — a notable political evolution.

4. **Legal framework changes**: The shift from fully proportional (2007, 2011) to mixed system (2016, 2021) reduced the number of PR seats from 450 to 225, changing the strategic calculus for the all-federal part.

---

**Caveats**: This synthesis is based on my training data. Some precise details (e.g., the exact number of candidates in the 2016 all-federal part beyond Medvedev) may benefit from verification against official CEC (Central Election Commission) records. The 2021 five-person list is very well documented and reliable.

key_facts: In the 2007 State Duma election, Vladimir Putin (then President) was placed as the sole candidate in United Russia's all-federal part of the federal list, an unprecedented 'locomotive' strategy. The party won 64.3% and 315 seats.
In the 2011 State Duma election, Dmitry Medvedev (then President) headed United Russia's all-federal part as a single locomotive figure. The party won 49.3% and 238 seats amid fraud allegations.
In the 2016 State Duma election (return to mixed system), Dmitry Medvedev (then PM) headed United Russia's all-federal part. The party won 54.2% of the list vote and 343 total seats.

In the 2021 State Duma election, United Russia shifted to a five-person all-federal part: Sergei Shoigu (Defence Minister), Sergei Lavrov (Foreign Minister), Denis Protsenko (Kommunarka Hospital chief physician), Anna Kuznetsova (Children's Rights Commissioner), and Elena Shmeleva (Talent and Success foundation head).
The 2021 shift to five candidates reflected Putin's decision not to lead the list personally and Medvedev's declining popularity after pension reform backlash.
None of the all-federal part 'locomotives' in any election cycle (2007-2021) actually took up Duma seats — they remained in their executive or other positions.
The legal cap on the all-federal part is up to 10 candidates under Russian electoral law.
United Russia won 49.8% of the party-list vote and 324 total seats in the 2021 election.
